problems.title: Griffiths 1.1
problems.statement: Using the geometric definitions of the dot product and cross product, prove that both operations are distributive. Do this first for three coplanar vectors and then for the general three-dimensional case.
problems.raw_statement: Using the geometric definitions of the dot product and cross product, prove that both operations are distributive. Do this first for three coplanar vectors and then for the general three-dimensional case.

problems.title: Griffiths 1.2
problems.statement: Decide whether the cross product is associative. Either prove that ( mathbf A times mathbf B) times mathbf C = mathbf A times ( mathbf B times mathbf C) always holds, or give a simple counterexample showing that it does not.
problems.raw_statement: Decide whether the cross product is associative. Either prove that \((\mathbf A \times \mathbf B)\times \mathbf C = \mathbf A \times (\mathbf B \times \mathbf C)\) always holds, or give a simple counterexample showing that it does not.

problems.title: Griffiths 1.3
problems.statement: Find the angle between two body diagonals of a cube.
problems.raw_statement: Find the angle between two body diagonals of a cube.

problems.title: Griffiths 1.4

problems.raw_statement: Use a cross product to determine the Cartesian components of the unit vector normal to the shaded plane in Fig.~1.11.

problems.title: Griffiths 1.5
problems.statement: Prove the BAC--CAB identity by expanding both sides in Cartesian components.
problems.raw_statement: Prove the BAC--CAB identity by expanding both sides in Cartesian components.

problems.title: Griffiths 1.6
problems.statement: Show that [ mathbf A times ( mathbf B times mathbf C)] + [ mathbf B times ( mathbf C times mathbf A)] + [ mathbf C times ( mathbf A times mathbf B)] = 0 . Then determine the conditions under which mathbf A times ( mathbf B times mathbf C) = ( mathbf A times mathbf B) times mathbf C .
problems.raw_statement: Show that \([\mathbf A \times (\mathbf B \times \mathbf C)] + [\mathbf B \times (\mathbf C \times \mathbf A)] + [\mathbf C \times (\mathbf A \times \mathbf B)] = 0\). Then determine the conditions under which \(\mathbf A \times (\mathbf B \times \mathbf C) = (\mathbf A \times \mathbf B)\times \mathbf C\).

problems.title: Griffiths 1.7
problems.statement: For a source point at (2,8,7) and a field point at (4,6,8) , compute the separation vector from source to field, its magnitude, and the corresponding unit vector.
problems.raw_statement: For a source point at \((2,8,7)\) and a field point at \((4,6,8)\), compute the separation vector from source to field, its magnitude, and the corresponding unit vector.

problems.title: Griffiths 1.8
problems.statement: (a) Show that the two-dimensional rotation matrix preserves dot products. (b) Determine the conditions the entries of a three-dimensional rotation matrix must satisfy in order to preserve the length of every vector.
problems.raw_statement: (a) Show that the two-dimensional rotation matrix preserves dot products. (b) Determine the conditions the entries of a three-dimensional rotation matrix must satisfy in order to preserve the length of every vector.

problems.title: Griffiths 1.9
problems.statement: Find the rotation matrix corresponding to a 120^ circ rotation about the axis through the origin and the point (1,1,1) , with the rotation taken clockwise when viewed along the axis toward the origin.
problems.raw_statement: Find the rotation matrix corresponding to a \(120^\circ\) rotation about the axis through the origin and the point \((1,1,1)\), with the rotation taken clockwise when viewed along the axis toward the origin.

problems.title: Griffiths 1.10
problems.statement: Study how vector quantities transform under changes of coordinates. (a) Find how vector components change under a translation. (b) Find how they change under inversion (x,y,z) mapsto (-x,-y,-z) . (c) Determine how a cross product transforms under inversion, decide whether the cross product of two pseudovectors is a vector or pseudovector, and name two classical-mechanics pseudovectors. (d) Determine how the scalar triple product transforms under inversion.
problems.raw_statement: Study how vector quantities transform under changes of coordinates. (a) Find how vector components change under a translation. (b) Find how they change under inversion \((x,y,z)\mapsto(-x,-y,-z)\). (c) Determine how a cross product transforms under inversion, decide whether the cross product of two pseudovectors is a vector or pseudovector, and name two classical-mechanics pseudovectors. (d) Determine how the scalar triple product transforms under inversion.

problems.title: Griffiths 1.11
problems.statement: Compute the gradient of each of the following scalar fields: (a) x^2+y^3+z^4 , (b) x^2y^3z^4 , and (c) e^x sin y ln z .

problems.statement: The height of a hill is h(x,y)=10(2xy-3x^2-4y^2-18x+28y+12) , where x and y are measured in miles east and north of South Hadley, and h is in feet. Find the hilltop location, the hilltop height, and the slope magnitude and direction at the point one mile east and one mile north of South Hadley.

Let \(\mathbf r\) be the separation vector from a fixed point \((x',y',z')\) to the point \((x,y,z)\), and let \(r=|\mathbf r|\). Show that (a) \(
abla(r^2)=2\mathbf r\), (b) \(
abla(1/r)=-\hat{\mathbf r}/r^2\), and (c) derive the corresponding general formula for \(

problems.title: Griffiths 1.62
problems.statement: The vector area of a surface is mathbf a= int _S d mathbf a . (a) Find the vector area of a hemispherical bowl of radius R . (b) Show that every closed surface has zero vector area. (c) Show that surfaces with the same boundary have the same vector area. (d) Prove mathbf a = frac 12 oint mathbf r times d mathbf l . (e) Show that oint ( mathbf c cdot mathbf r)\,d mathbf l = mathbf a times mathbf c for constant mathbf c .
problems.raw_statement: The vector area of a surface is \(\mathbf a=\int_S d\mathbf a\). (a) Find the vector area of a hemispherical bowl of radius \(R\). (b) Show that every closed surface has zero vector area. (c) Show that surfaces with the same boundary have the same vector area. (d) Prove \(\mathbf a = \frac12\oint \mathbf r\times d\mathbf l\). (e) Show that \(\oint (\mathbf c\cdot\mathbf r)\,d\mathbf l = \mathbf a\times\mathbf c\) for constant \(\mathbf c\).

problems.title: Griffiths 1.63
problems.statement: For the field r^n mathbf r : (a) compute its divergence directly, test the result with the divergence theorem, decide whether a delta function is needed at the origin, and derive the general formula for nabla cdot (r^n mathbf r ) ; (b) compute its curl and verify the conclusion using Problem 1.61(b).
problems.raw_statement: For the field \(r^n\hat{\mathbf r}\): (a) compute its divergence directly, test the result with the divergence theorem, decide whether a delta function is needed at the origin, and derive the general formula for \(\nabla\cdot(r^n\hat{\mathbf r})\); (b) compute its curl and verify the conclusion using Problem 1.61(b).

problems.title: Griffiths 1.64
problems.statement: Define D(r, epsilon )=-(1/4 pi ) nabla ^2[1/ r^2+ epsilon ^2 ] . Show that as epsilon to 0 , this quantity approaches delta ^3( mathbf r) by proving: (a) an explicit formula for D(r, epsilon ) , (b) D(0, epsilon ) to infty , (c) D(r, epsilon ) to 0 for r neq 0 , and (d) the integral of D(r, epsilon ) over all space is always 1 .
problems.raw_statement: Define \(D(r,\epsilon)=-(1/4\pi)\nabla^2[1/\sqrt{r^2+\epsilon^2}]\). Show that as \(\epsilon\to0\), this quantity approaches \(\delta^3(\mathbf r)\) by proving: (a) an explicit formula for \(D(r,\epsilon)\), (b) \(D(0,\epsilon)\to\infty\), (c) \(D(r,\epsilon)\to0\) for \(r\neq0\), and (d) the integral of \(D(r,\epsilon)\) over all space is always \(1\).

problems.title: Griffiths 2.1
problems.statement: Place twelve equal charges q at the vertices of a regular 12-gon and a test charge Q at the center. (a) Find the net force on Q . (b) Remove the charge at ``6 o'clock'' and find the new force on Q , explaining the symmetry argument carefully. (c) Repeat the central-force question for 13 equal charges on a regular 13-gon. (d) Remove one of those 13 charges and determine the resulting force on Q .
problems.raw_statement: Place twelve equal charges \(q\) at the vertices of a regular 12-gon and a test charge \(Q\) at the center. (a) Find the net force on \(Q\). (b) Remove the charge at ``6 o'clock'' and find the new force on \(Q\), explaining the symmetry argument carefully. (c) Repeat the central-force question for 13 equal charges on a regular 13-gon. (d) Remove one of those 13 charges and determine the resulting force on \(Q\).

problems.raw_statement: Find the electric field at a point a height \(z\) above the midpoint of two equal and opposite point charges \(\pm q\) separated by a distance \(d\). Give both magnitude and direction.

problems.raw_statement: A finite straight segment of length \(L\) carries uniform line charge density \(\lambda\). Find the electric field at a point a height \(z\) above one end of the segment, and check that the result behaves sensibly in the limit \(z\gg L\).

problems.statement: Find the electric field at a point a height z above the center of a square loop of side a carrying uniform line charge density lambda .
problems.raw_statement: Find the electric field at a point a height \(z\) above the center of a square loop of side \(a\) carrying uniform line charge density \(\lambda\).

problems.statement: Find the electric field at a point a height z above the center of a circular loop of radius r carrying uniform line charge density lambda .
problems.raw_statement: Find the electric field at a point a height \(z\) above the center of a circular loop of radius \(r\) carrying uniform line charge density \(\lambda\).

problems.title: Griffiths 2.6

problems.raw_statement: Find the electric field at a point a height \(z\) above the center of a uniformly charged disk of radius \(R\) and surface charge density \(\sigma\). Check the limits \(R\to\infty\) and \(z\gg R\).

problems.title: Griffiths 2.7

problems.raw_statement: A spherical shell of radius \(R\) carries uniform surface charge density \(\sigma\). Compute the electric field both inside and outside the shell, express the answer in terms of the total charge \(q\), and handle carefully the cases \(z<R\) and \(z>R\).

problems.title: Griffiths 2.8

problems.statement: The potential of some charge distribution is V(r)=A e^ - lambda r /r . Determine the electric field, the charge density, and the total charge of the configuration.
problems.raw_statement: The potential of some charge distribution is \(V(r)=A e^{-\lambda r}/r\). Determine the electric field, the charge density, and the total charge of the configuration.

problems.title: Griffiths 2.51
problems.statement: Find the potential at a point on the rim of a uniformly charged disk of radius R and surface charge density sigma . First reduce the answer to a dimensionless integral for a constant k , and then evaluate k analytically if possible or numerically if necessary.
problems.raw_statement: Find the potential at a point on the rim of a uniformly charged disk of radius \(R\) and surface charge density \(\sigma\). First reduce the answer to a dimensionless integral for a constant \(k\), and then evaluate \(k\) analytically if possible or numerically if necessary.

problems.title: Griffiths 2.52
problems.statement: Two infinitely long wires parallel to the x -axis carry line charges + lambda and - lambda . (a) Find the potential at any point (x,y,z) , taking the origin as reference. (b) Show that the equipotentials are circular cylinders and determine the axis and radius corresponding to a given potential V_0 .
problems.raw_statement: Two infinitely long wires parallel to the \(x\)-axis carry line charges \(+\lambda\) and \(-\lambda\). (a) Find the potential at any point \((x,y,z)\), taking the origin as reference. (b) Show that the equipotentials are circular cylinders and determine the axis and radius corresponding to a given potential \(V_0\).

problems.title: Griffiths 2.53
problems.statement: Analyze the space-charge-limited planar vacuum diode. Electrons start at a cathode held at zero potential and accelerate toward an anode at potential V_0 . Assuming the plate area is large compared with the gap, so all quantities depend only on x : (a) write Poisson's equation, (b) relate electron speed to V(x) , (c) use steady current to relate rho and v , (d) eliminate rho and v to get a differential equation for V , (e) solve for V(x) , rho (x) , and v(x) , and compare V(x) to the empty-gap potential, and (f) derive the Child--Langmuir law I=K V_0^ 3/2 and determine K .
problems.raw_statement: Analyze the space-charge-limited planar vacuum diode. Electrons start at a cathode held at zero potential and accelerate toward an anode at potential \(V_0\). Assuming the plate area is large compared with the gap, so all quantities depend only on \(x\): (a) write Poisson's equation, (b) relate electron speed to \(V(x)\), (c) use steady current to relate \(\rho\) and \(v\), (d) eliminate \(\rho\) and \(v\) to get a differential equation for \(V\), (e) solve for \(V(x)\), \(\rho(x)\), and \(v(x)\), and compare \(V(x)\) to the empty-gap potential, and (f) derive the Child--Langmuir law \(I=K V_0^{3/2}\) and determine \(K\).

problems.title: Griffiths 2.54
problems.statement: Assume Coulomb's law is corrected so that the force between point charges becomes mathbf F = (1/4 pi epsilon _0)\, q_1q_2\, e^ -r/ lambda (1+r/ lambda ) mathbf r /r^2 , with lambda a huge new length scale. Reformulate electrostatics under this law, assuming superposition still holds: (a) write the field of a continuous charge distribution, (b) determine whether a scalar potential still exists and justify your answer, (c) find the point-charge potential using infinity as reference, (d) derive the modified integral law for a sphere centered on a point charge, (e) generalize it to arbitrary charge distributions, (f) redraw the usual rho - mathbf E - V relation diagram for this modified theory, and (g) show that charge on a conductor now splits between the volume and the surface.
problems.raw_statement: Assume Coulomb's law is corrected so that the force between point charges becomes \(\mathbf F = (1/4\pi\epsilon_0)\, q_1q_2\, e^{-r/\lambda}(1+r/\lambda)\hat{\mathbf r}/r^2\), with \(\lambda\) a huge new length scale. Reformulate electrostatics under this law, assuming superposition still holds: (a) write the field of a continuous charge distribution, (b) determine whether a scalar potential still exists and justify your answer, (c) find the point-charge potential using infinity as reference, (d) derive the modified integral law for a sphere centered on a point charge, (e) generalize it to arbitrary charge distributions, (f) redraw the usual \(\rho\)-\(\mathbf E\)-\(V\) relation diagram for this modified theory, and (g) show that charge on a conductor now splits between the volume and the surface.

problems.title: Griffiths 2.55
problems.statement: Suppose the electric field is mathbf E=(ax,0,0) , where a is constant. Find the charge density, and explain how the field can point everywhere in the same Cartesian direction even though the charge density is uniform.
problems.raw_statement: Suppose the electric field is \(\mathbf E=(ax,0,0)\), where \(a\) is constant. Find the charge density, and explain how the field can point everywhere in the same Cartesian direction even though the charge density is uniform.

problems.title: Griffiths 2.56
problems.statement: Construct the gravitational analog of electrostatics for a uniform sphere of mass M and radius R . Compute its gravitational self-energy, estimate the gravitational energy of the Sun from observed parameters, and compare the implied lifetime from gravitational radiation power alone with the actual age of the Sun.
problems.raw_statement: Construct the gravitational analog of electrostatics for a uniform sphere of mass \(M\) and radius \(R\). Compute its gravitational self-energy, estimate the gravitational energy of the Sun from observed parameters, and compare the implied lifetime from gravitational radiation power alone with the actual age of the Sun.

problems.title: Griffiths 2.57
problems.statement: Use the known surface-charge formula for a charged ellipsoid to generate three limiting conductor problems by suitable choices of a , b , and c : (a) the net surface charge density on a circular conducting disk of radius R , (b) the net surface charge density on an infinite conducting ribbon extending from x=-a to x=+a , and (c) the charge per unit length on a conducting needle running from x=-a to x=+a . Sketch each resulting distribution.
problems.raw_statement: Use the known surface-charge formula for a charged ellipsoid to generate three limiting conductor problems by suitable choices of \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\): (a) the net surface charge density on a circular conducting disk of radius \(R\), (b) the net surface charge density on an infinite conducting ribbon extending from \(x=-a\) to \(x=+a\), and (c) the charge per unit length on a conducting needle running from \(x=-a\) to \(x=+a\). Sketch each resulting distribution.

problems.title: Griffiths 2.58
problems.statement: (a) Three equal point charges q sit at the vertices of an equilateral triangle inscribed in a circle of radius a . Besides the center, there are three other points inside the triangle where the field vanishes. Locate them numerically. (b) For a regular n -gon of equal charges, there are n additional interior zero-field points. Find their radial positions for n=4 and n=5 , and discuss what seems to happen as n to infty .
problems.raw_statement: (a) Three equal point charges \(q\) sit at the vertices of an equilateral triangle inscribed in a circle of radius \(a\). Besides the center, there are three other points inside the triangle where the field vanishes. Locate them numerically. (b) For a regular \(n\)-gon of equal charges, there are \(n\) additional interior zero-field points. Find their radial positions for \(n=4\) and \(n=5\), and discuss what seems to happen as \(n\to\infty\).

problems.title: Griffiths 2.59
problems.statement: Decide whether the following theorem is true: if a charged conductor experiences force mathbf F in an external field mathbf E_e , then reversing the external field to - mathbf E_e reverses the force to - mathbf F . Either prove the claim or give a counterexample. Then reconsider the question under the additional assumption that the external field is uniform.
problems.raw_statement: Decide whether the following theorem is true: if a charged conductor experiences force \(\mathbf F\) in an external field \(\mathbf E_e\), then reversing the external field to \(-\mathbf E_e\) reverses the force to \(-\mathbf F\). Either prove the claim or give a counterexample. Then reconsider the question under the additional assumption that the external field is uniform.

problems.title: Griffiths 2.60
problems.statement: A point charge q sits at the center of an uncharged spherical conducting shell with inner radius a and outer radius b . How much work is required to remove the charge to infinity through a tiny hole in the shell?
problems.raw_statement: A point charge \(q\) sits at the center of an uncharged spherical conducting shell with inner radius \(a\) and outer radius \(b\). How much work is required to remove the charge to infinity through a tiny hole in the shell?

problems.title: Griffiths 2.61
problems.statement: For N equal point charges constrained to lie on or inside a circle of radius R , determine the minimum-energy configuration. In particular, show numerically that for N=12 the lowest-energy state has 11 charges on the boundary and one at the center, and decide whether the N=11 minimum has all 11 on the boundary or 10 on the boundary plus one at the center. Express energies in units of q^2/(4 pi epsilon _0 R) .
problems.raw_statement: For \(N\) equal point charges constrained to lie on or inside a circle of radius \(R\), determine the minimum-energy configuration. In particular, show numerically that for \(N=12\) the lowest-energy state has 11 charges on the boundary and one at the center, and decide whether the \(N=11\) minimum has all 11 on the boundary or 10 on the boundary plus one at the center. Express energies in units of \(q^2/(4\pi\epsilon_0 R)\).

problems.title: Griffiths 3.1
problems.statement: Compute the average electrostatic potential over a spherical surface of radius R produced by a point charge q located inside that sphere. Then show the more general formula V_ ave = V_ center + Q_ enc /(4 pi epsilon _0 R) , where V_ center is the contribution at the center from all charges outside the sphere and Q_ enc is the total enclosed charge.
problems.raw_statement: Compute the average electrostatic potential over a spherical surface of radius \(R\) produced by a point charge \(q\) located inside that sphere. Then show the more general formula \(V_{\mathrm{ave}} = V_{\mathrm{center}} + Q_{\mathrm{enc}}/(4\pi\epsilon_0 R)\), where \(V_{\mathrm{center}}\) is the contribution at the center from all charges outside the sphere and \(Q_{\mathrm{enc}}\) is the total enclosed charge.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.2
problems.statement: Give a one-sentence justification of Earnshaw's theorem: a charged particle cannot be held in stable equilibrium by electrostatic forces alone. Then apply that reasoning to the cube of fixed charges discussed in the chapter and explain where the apparent ``electrostatic bottle'' fails.
problems.raw_statement: Give a one-sentence justification of Earnshaw's theorem: a charged particle cannot be held in stable equilibrium by electrostatic forces alone. Then apply that reasoning to the cube of fixed charges discussed in the chapter and explain where the apparent ``electrostatic bottle'' fails.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.3
problems.statement: Solve Laplace's equation in spherical coordinates for the special case V = V(r) only. Do the analogous calculation in cylindrical coordinates for the case V = V(s) only.
problems.raw_statement: Solve Laplace's equation in spherical coordinates for the special case \(V = V(r)\) only. Do the analogous calculation in cylindrical coordinates for the case \(V = V(s)\) only.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.4
problems.statement: (a) Show that the average electric field over a spherical surface due to charges outside the sphere equals the field at the center. (b) Determine the corresponding average field due to charges located inside the sphere.
problems.raw_statement: (a) Show that the average electric field over a spherical surface due to charges outside the sphere equals the field at the center. (b) Determine the corresponding average field due to charges located inside the sphere.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.5
problems.statement: Prove that the electrostatic field is uniquely determined when the charge density rho is specified in the volume and, on each boundary surface, either the potential V or the normal derivative partial V/ partial n is given. Do not assume the boundaries are conductors or equipotentials.
problems.raw_statement: Prove that the electrostatic field is uniquely determined when the charge density \(\rho\) is specified in the volume and, on each boundary surface, either the potential \(V\) or the normal derivative \(\partial V/\partial n\) is given. Do not assume the boundaries are conductors or equipotentials.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.6
problems.statement: Re-derive the second uniqueness theorem using Green's identity, taking T = U = V_3 , and fill in the missing steps of the proof.
problems.raw_statement: Re-derive the second uniqueness theorem using Green's identity, taking \(T = U = V_3\), and fill in the missing steps of the proof.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.7
problems.statement: A grounded conducting plane lies in the xy -plane. Two point charges are above it on the same vertical line: a charge +q at height 3d and a charge -2q at height d . Find the net force on the charge +q .
problems.raw_statement: A grounded conducting plane lies in the \(xy\)-plane. Two point charges are above it on the same vertical line: a charge \(+q\) at height \(3d\) and a charge \(-2q\) at height \(d\). Find the net force on the charge \(+q\).

problems.title: Griffiths 3.8
problems.statement: For the image solution of a point charge outside a grounded conducting sphere: (a) rewrite the potential in spherical coordinates using the law of cosines, (b) find the induced surface charge density sigma ( theta ) and integrate it to obtain the total induced charge, and (c) compute the electrostatic energy of the configuration.
problems.raw_statement: For the image solution of a point charge outside a grounded conducting sphere: (a) rewrite the potential in spherical coordinates using the law of cosines, (b) find the induced surface charge density \(\sigma(\theta)\) and integrate it to obtain the total induced charge, and (c) compute the electrostatic energy of the configuration.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.9
problems.statement: Modify the image-charge construction for a conducting sphere that is held at a fixed nonzero potential V_0 rather than grounded. Determine what additional image charge is needed and where it must be placed. Then use the result to find the force between a point charge q and a neutral conducting sphere.
problems.raw_statement: Modify the image-charge construction for a conducting sphere that is held at a fixed nonzero potential \(V_0\) rather than grounded. Determine what additional image charge is needed and where it must be placed. Then use the result to find the force between a point charge \(q\) and a neutral conducting sphere.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.10
problems.statement: An infinite straight wire carrying uniform line charge density lambda runs parallel to the x -axis at height d above a grounded conducting plane. (a) Find the potential in the region above the plane. (b) Determine the induced surface charge density on the conductor.
problems.raw_statement: An infinite straight wire carrying uniform line charge density \(\lambda\) runs parallel to the \(x\)-axis at height \(d\) above a grounded conducting plane. (a) Find the potential in the region above the plane. (b) Determine the induced surface charge density on the conductor.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.11
problems.statement: Two grounded conducting half-planes meet at right angles. A point charge q is placed in the wedge between them. Construct the necessary image charges, find the potential in the physical region, compute the force on q , and determine the work required to bring the charge in from infinity. Then discuss for which wedge angles the method of images still works.
problems.raw_statement: Two grounded conducting half-planes meet at right angles. A point charge \(q\) is placed in the wedge between them. Construct the necessary image charges, find the potential in the physical region, compute the force on \(q\), and determine the work required to bring the charge in from infinity. Then discuss for which wedge angles the method of images still works.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.12
problems.statement: Two infinitely long conducting cylinders of radius R have centers separated by distance 2d . One cylinder is held at potential +V_0 and the other at -V_0 . Find the electrostatic potential everywhere.
problems.raw_statement: Two infinitely long conducting cylinders of radius \(R\) have centers separated by distance \(2d\). One cylinder is held at potential \(+V_0\) and the other at \(-V_0\). Find the electrostatic potential everywhere.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.13
problems.statement: In the infinite slot problem of Example 3.3, replace the boundary at x=0 by two conducting strips: 0 le y le a/2 is kept at +V_0 , while a/2 le y le a is kept at -V_0 . Find the potential inside the slot.
problems.raw_statement: In the infinite slot problem of Example 3.3, replace the boundary at \(x=0\) by two conducting strips: \(0 \le y \le a/2\) is kept at \(+V_0\), while \(a/2 \le y \le a\) is kept at \(-V_0\). Find the potential inside the slot.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.14
problems.statement: For the infinite slot of Example 3.3 with the strip at x=0 held at constant potential V_0 , determine the induced charge density sigma (y) on that strip.
problems.raw_statement: For the infinite slot of Example 3.3 with the strip at \(x=0\) held at constant potential \(V_0\), determine the induced charge density \(\sigma(y)\) on that strip.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.15
problems.statement: A rectangular pipe extends infinitely along the z -direction. The sides y=0 , y=a , and x=0 are grounded, while the side x=b is held at a prescribed potential V_0(y) . (a) Derive a general formula for the potential inside the pipe. (b) Specialize to the case V_0(y)=V_0 constant.
problems.raw_statement: A rectangular pipe extends infinitely along the \(z\)-direction. The sides \(y=0\), \(y=a\), and \(x=0\) are grounded, while the side \(x=b\) is held at a prescribed potential \(V_0(y)\). (a) Derive a general formula for the potential inside the pipe. (b) Specialize to the case \(V_0(y)=V_0\) constant.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.16
problems.statement: A cube of side a has five grounded faces; the sixth face is insulated from the others and maintained at constant potential V_0 . Find the potential inside the cube. Also infer, by symmetry or superposition, what the potential at the center should be and check that your formula is consistent with it.
problems.raw_statement: A cube of side \(a\) has five grounded faces; the sixth face is insulated from the others and maintained at constant potential \(V_0\). Find the potential inside the cube. Also infer, by symmetry or superposition, what the potential at the center should be and check that your formula is consistent with it.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.17
problems.statement: Use Rodrigues' formula to derive P_3(x) . Verify directly that P_3( cos theta ) satisfies the angular differential equation for l=3 , and check by explicit integration that P_3 and P_1 are orthogonal.
problems.raw_statement: Use Rodrigues' formula to derive \(P_3(x)\). Verify directly that \(P_3(\cos\theta)\) satisfies the angular differential equation for \(l=3\), and check by explicit integration that \(P_3\) and \(P_1\) are orthogonal.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.18
problems.statement: (a) Suppose the surface of a sphere is held at constant potential V_0 . Use the spherical separation-of-variables machinery to find the potential inside and outside the sphere. (b) Using the same method, find the potential inside and outside a spherical shell carrying uniform surface charge density sigma _0 .
problems.raw_statement: (a) Suppose the surface of a sphere is held at constant potential \(V_0\). Use the spherical separation-of-variables machinery to find the potential inside and outside the sphere. (b) Using the same method, find the potential inside and outside a spherical shell carrying uniform surface charge density \(\sigma_0\).

problems.title: Griffiths 3.19
problems.statement: A sphere of radius R has prescribed surface potential V(R, theta )=k cos (3 theta ) , with no charge present either inside or outside. Find the potential in both regions and determine the resulting surface charge density sigma ( theta ) .
problems.raw_statement: A sphere of radius \(R\) has prescribed surface potential \(V(R,\theta)=k\cos(3\theta)\), with no charge present either inside or outside. Find the potential in both regions and determine the resulting surface charge density \(\sigma(\theta)\).

problems.title: Griffiths 3.20
problems.statement: Let the potential V_0( theta ) be specified on the surface of a sphere, with no free charge inside or outside. Show that the surface charge density can be written as a Legendre-series expression in terms of the expansion coefficients of V_0( theta ) .
problems.raw_statement: Let the potential \(V_0(\theta)\) be specified on the surface of a sphere, with no free charge inside or outside. Show that the surface charge density can be written as a Legendre-series expression in terms of the expansion coefficients of \(V_0(\theta)\).

problems.title: Griffiths 3.21
problems.statement: A conducting sphere of radius R carries total charge Q and is placed in an otherwise uniform external field E _0 . Find the potential outside the sphere and state clearly what reference level you are using for the potential.
problems.raw_statement: A conducting sphere of radius \(R\) carries total charge \(Q\) and is placed in an otherwise uniform external field \(\mathbf{E}_0\). Find the potential outside the sphere and state clearly what reference level you are using for the potential.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.22
problems.statement: Starting from the known on-axis potential of a uniformly charged disk, derive the first three terms of the Legendre expansion for the off-axis potential when r>R . Then obtain the interior expansion for r<R , treating the upper and lower hemispherical regions separately.
problems.raw_statement: Starting from the known on-axis potential of a uniformly charged disk, derive the first three terms of the Legendre expansion for the off-axis potential when \(r>R\). Then obtain the interior expansion for \(r<R\), treating the upper and lower hemispherical regions separately.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.23
problems.statement: A spherical shell of radius R carries surface charge + sigma _0 on the northern hemisphere and - sigma _0 on the southern hemisphere. Find the potentials inside and outside the shell, working out the expansion coefficients explicitly through A_6 and B_6 .
problems.raw_statement: A spherical shell of radius \(R\) carries surface charge \(+\sigma_0\) on the northern hemisphere and \(-\sigma_0\) on the southern hemisphere. Find the potentials inside and outside the shell, working out the expansion coefficients explicitly through \(A_6\) and \(B_6\).

problems.title: Griffiths 3.24
problems.statement: Solve Laplace's equation in cylindrical coordinates by separation of variables under the assumption of no z -dependence. Be sure to include all radial solutions, including the one needed to represent the potential of an infinite line charge.
problems.raw_statement: Solve Laplace's equation in cylindrical coordinates by separation of variables under the assumption of no \(z\)-dependence. Be sure to include all radial solutions, including the one needed to represent the potential of an infinite line charge.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.25
problems.statement: An infinitely long conducting cylinder of radius R is placed in a uniform electric field E _0 perpendicular to its axis. Using the cylindrical solutions from Problem 3.24, find the external potential and the induced surface charge density.
problems.raw_statement: An infinitely long conducting cylinder of radius \(R\) is placed in a uniform electric field \(\mathbf{E}_0\) perpendicular to its axis. Using the cylindrical solutions from Problem 3.24, find the external potential and the induced surface charge density.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.26
problems.statement: A surface charge density sigma ( phi )=a sin (5 phi ) is fixed on an infinite cylindrical shell of radius R . Determine the electrostatic potential inside and outside the cylinder.
problems.raw_statement: A surface charge density \(\sigma(\phi)=a\sin(5\phi)\) is fixed on an infinite cylindrical shell of radius \(R\). Determine the electrostatic potential inside and outside the cylinder.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.27
problems.statement: A sphere of radius R centered at the origin carries the axially symmetric volume charge density rho (r, theta )=k\,r^2(R-2r) sin theta , where k is a constant. Find the far-field approximation to the potential on the z -axis.
problems.raw_statement: A sphere of radius \(R\) centered at the origin carries the axially symmetric volume charge density \(\rho(r,\theta)=k\,r^2(R-2r)\sin\theta\), where \(k\) is a constant. Find the far-field approximation to the potential on the \(z\)-axis.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.28
problems.statement: A circular ring of radius R in the xy -plane carries uniform line charge density lambda . Find the first three terms, n=0,1,2 , in the multipole expansion of the potential V(r, theta ) .
problems.raw_statement: A circular ring of radius \(R\) in the \(xy\)-plane carries uniform line charge density \(\lambda\). Find the first three terms, \(n=0,1,2\), in the multipole expansion of the potential \(V(r,\theta)\).

problems.title: Griffiths 3.29
problems.statement: Four point charges are each a distance a from the origin: 3q lies on the +z -axis, q lies on the +y -axis, and two charges -2q lie on the pm x -axis. Find a simple far-field approximation for the potential, expressed in spherical coordinates.
problems.raw_statement: Four point charges are each a distance \(a\) from the origin: \(3q\) lies on the \(+z\)-axis, \(q\) lies on the \(+y\)-axis, and two charges \(-2q\) lie on the \(\pm x\)-axis. Find a simple far-field approximation for the potential, expressed in spherical coordinates.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.30
problems.statement: For the spherical shell with surface charge density sigma = k cos theta : (a) compute the dipole moment of the shell, and (b) compare the exact potential with the far-field dipole approximation to determine whether any higher multipole moments are present.
problems.raw_statement: For the spherical shell with surface charge density \(\sigma = k\cos\theta\): (a) compute the dipole moment of the shell, and (b) compare the exact potential with the far-field dipole approximation to determine whether any higher multipole moments are present.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.31
problems.statement: For the physical dipole of Example 3.10, expand 1/r_ pm through order (d/r)^3 and use the result to identify the quadrupole and octopole contributions to the potential.
problems.raw_statement: For the physical dipole of Example 3.10, expand \(1/r_{\pm}\) through order \((d/r)^3\) and use the result to identify the quadrupole and octopole contributions to the potential.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.32
problems.statement: Two point charges, 3q and -q , are separated by distance a . For each of the three arrangements shown in Fig. 3.35, compute the monopole moment, the dipole moment, and the large- r potential including both the monopole and dipole terms.
problems.raw_statement: Two point charges, \(3q\) and \(-q\), are separated by distance \(a\). For each of the three arrangements shown in Fig.~3.35, compute the monopole moment, the dipole moment, and the large-\(r\) potential including both the monopole and dipole terms.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.33
problems.statement: An ideal dipole p =p hat z is fixed at the origin. Find the force on a point charge q placed at (a) (a,0,0) and (b) (0,0,a) . Then determine the work required to move the charge from (a,0,0) to (0,0,a) .
problems.raw_statement: An ideal dipole \(\mathbf{p}=p\hat{\mathbf{z}}\) is fixed at the origin. Find the force on a point charge \(q\) placed at (a) \((a,0,0)\) and (b) \((0,0,a)\). Then determine the work required to move the charge from \((a,0,0)\) to \((0,0,a)\).

problems.title: Griffiths 3.34
problems.statement: Three point charges lie at distance a from the origin in the arrangement of Fig. 3.38. Find the far-field electric field in spherical coordinates, keeping the two lowest nonvanishing orders in the multipole expansion.
problems.raw_statement: Three point charges lie at distance \(a\) from the origin in the arrangement of Fig.~3.38. Find the far-field electric field in spherical coordinates, keeping the two lowest nonvanishing orders in the multipole expansion.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.35
problems.statement: A solid sphere of radius R is centered at the origin. The northern hemisphere carries uniform volume charge density + rho _0 , and the southern hemisphere carries - rho _0 . Find the approximate field E (r, theta ) for r gg R .
problems.raw_statement: A solid sphere of radius \(R\) is centered at the origin. The northern hemisphere carries uniform volume charge density \(+\rho_0\), and the southern hemisphere carries \(-\rho_0\). Find the approximate field \(\mathbf{E}(r,\theta)\) for \(r \gg R\).

problems.title: Griffiths 3.36
problems.statement: Show that the electric field of an ideal dipole can be written in coordinate-free form as E _ dip ( r ) = (1/4 pi epsilon _0 r^3)\,[3( p cdot hat r ) hat r - p ] .
problems.raw_statement: Show that the electric field of an ideal dipole can be written in coordinate-free form as \(\mathbf{E}_{\mathrm{dip}}(\mathbf{r}) = (1/4\pi\epsilon_0 r^3)\,[3(\mathbf{p}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{r}})\hat{\mathbf{r}}-\mathbf{p}]\).

problems.title: Griffiths 3.37
problems.statement: Give an alternative proof of the spherical averaging theorem using only Laplace's equation. Let V_ ave (R) be the average of the potential over a sphere of radius R centered at a point P , derive dV_ ave /dR in terms of a flux integral, and use the divergence theorem to show that V_ ave (R)=V(P) in a charge-free region.
problems.raw_statement: Give an alternative proof of the spherical averaging theorem using only Laplace's equation. Let \(V_{\mathrm{ave}}(R)\) be the average of the potential over a sphere of radius \(R\) centered at a point \(P\), derive \(dV_{\mathrm{ave}}/dR\) in terms of a flux integral, and use the divergence theorem to show that \(V_{\mathrm{ave}}(R)=V(P)\) in a charge-free region.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.38
problems.statement: Re-derive the induced surface charge density on a grounded conducting plane due to a point charge q at height d , but do so without the method of images. Write the normal field just below the conductor as the sum of the field from q and the field from the unknown surface charge density sigma (x,y) , set the total to zero, and solve for sigma .
problems.raw_statement: Re-derive the induced surface charge density on a grounded conducting plane due to a point charge \(q\) at height \(d\), but do so without the method of images. Write the normal field just below the conductor as the sum of the field from \(q\) and the field from the unknown surface charge density \(\sigma(x,y)\), set the total to zero, and solve for \(\sigma\).

problems.title: Griffiths 3.39
problems.statement: Two infinite grounded conducting planes are parallel and separated by distance a . A point charge q lies between them, at distance x from one plate. Find the force on the charge and check the limits a to infty and x=a/2 .
problems.raw_statement: Two infinite grounded conducting planes are parallel and separated by distance \(a\). A point charge \(q\) lies between them, at distance \(x\) from one plate. Find the force on the charge and check the limits \(a\to\infty\) and \(x=a/2\).

problems.title: Griffiths 3.40
problems.statement: Two infinitely long straight wires carrying line charges + lambda and - lambda lie on opposite sides of a long conducting cylinder of radius R . The wires are each at distance a from the cylinder axis, and the cylinder carries no net charge. Find the potential everywhere.
problems.raw_statement: Two infinitely long straight wires carrying line charges \(+\lambda\) and \(-\lambda\) lie on opposite sides of a long conducting cylinder of radius \(R\). The wires are each at distance \(a\) from the cylinder axis, and the cylinder carries no net charge. Find the potential everywhere.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.41
problems.statement: Approximate the C _ 60 ^ - ion as a conducting spherical shell of radius R=3.5\, AA carrying one extra electron smeared uniformly over its surface. A second electron experiences attraction at short distance but repulsion far away. (a) Find numerically the radius r where the net force on the second electron vanishes. (b) Find the energy required to bring an electron from infinity to that radius.
problems.raw_statement: Approximate the \( \mathrm{C}_{60}^{-} \) ion as a conducting spherical shell of radius \(R=3.5\,\AA\) carrying one extra electron smeared uniformly over its surface. A second electron experiences attraction at short distance but repulsion far away. (a) Find numerically the radius \(r\) where the net force on the second electron vanishes. (b) Find the energy required to bring an electron from infinity to that radius.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.42
problems.statement: Use superposition of separated solutions to find the potential in a rectangular pipe for which the two faces x= pm b are held at V_0 , the face y=a is held at V_1 , and the face y=0 is grounded.
problems.raw_statement: Use superposition of separated solutions to find the potential in a rectangular pipe for which the two faces \(x=\pm b\) are held at \(V_0\), the face \(y=a\) is held at \(V_1\), and the face \(y=0\) is grounded.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.43
problems.statement: A conducting sphere of radius a , held at potential V_0 , is surrounded by a thin concentric spherical shell of radius b carrying surface charge density sigma ( theta )=k cos theta . (a) Find the potential in the regions r>b and a<r<b . (b) Determine the induced surface charge density on the inner conductor. (c) Find the total charge of the system and check that it matches the large- r behavior of the potential.
problems.raw_statement: A conducting sphere of radius \(a\), held at potential \(V_0\), is surrounded by a thin concentric spherical shell of radius \(b\) carrying surface charge density \(\sigma(\theta)=k\cos\theta\). (a) Find the potential in the regions \(r>b\) and \(a<r<b\). (b) Determine the induced surface charge density on the inner conductor. (c) Find the total charge of the system and check that it matches the large-\(r\) behavior of the potential.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.44
problems.statement: A total charge +Q is distributed uniformly along the segment of the z -axis from -a to +a . Derive the multipole expansion of the potential for r>a , showing that only even Legendre polynomials appear and obtaining the first few terms explicitly.
problems.raw_statement: A total charge \(+Q\) is distributed uniformly along the segment of the \(z\)-axis from \(-a\) to \(+a\). Derive the multipole expansion of the potential for \(r>a\), showing that only even Legendre polynomials appear and obtaining the first few terms explicitly.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.45
problems.statement: A long cylindrical shell of radius R carries uniform surface charge + sigma _0 on its upper half and - sigma _0 on its lower half. Find the electrostatic potential both inside and outside the cylinder.
problems.raw_statement: A long cylindrical shell of radius \(R\) carries uniform surface charge \(+\sigma_0\) on its upper half and \(-\sigma_0\) on its lower half. Find the electrostatic potential both inside and outside the cylinder.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.46
problems.statement: A thin insulating rod runs along the z -axis from z=-a to z=+a . In each of the cases lambda (z)=k cos ( pi z/2a) , lambda (z)=k sin ( pi z/a) , and lambda (z)=k cos ( pi z/a) , determine the leading nonvanishing term in the multipole expansion of the potential.
problems.raw_statement: A thin insulating rod runs along the \(z\)-axis from \(z=-a\) to \(z=+a\). In each of the cases \(\lambda(z)=k\cos(\pi z/2a)\), \(\lambda(z)=k\sin(\pi z/a)\), and \(\lambda(z)=k\cos(\pi z/a)\), determine the leading nonvanishing term in the multipole expansion of the potential.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.47
problems.statement: Show that the average electric field over the volume of a sphere of radius R , due to the charge contained inside that sphere, is E _ ave =- p /(4 pi epsilon _0 R^3) , where p is the total dipole moment of the enclosed charge distribution. Follow the suggested route: first analyze a single interior point charge by comparison with a uniformly charged sphere, then generalize by superposition, and also show that the average field from exterior charges equals the field at the center.
problems.raw_statement: Show that the average electric field over the volume of a sphere of radius \(R\), due to the charge contained inside that sphere, is \(\mathbf{E}_{\mathrm{ave}}=-\mathbf{p}/(4\pi\epsilon_0 R^3)\), where \(\mathbf{p}\) is the total dipole moment of the enclosed charge distribution. Follow the suggested route: first analyze a single interior point charge by comparison with a uniformly charged sphere, then generalize by superposition, and also show that the average field from exterior charges equals the field at the center.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.48
problems.statement: (a) Compute the volume average of the dipole field over a sphere centered at the dipole and compare it with the general averaging theorem. (b) Use that comparison to infer the singular field contribution at the origin, thereby recovering the delta-function term in the exact field of an ideal dipole.
problems.raw_statement: (a) Compute the volume average of the dipole field over a sphere centered at the dipole and compare it with the general averaging theorem. (b) Use that comparison to infer the singular field contribution at the origin, thereby recovering the delta-function term in the exact field of an ideal dipole.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.49
problems.statement: For the spherical shell with sigma ( theta )=k cos theta , take the limit R to 0 and show that the result reproduces the delta-function term in the exact dipole field.
problems.raw_statement: For the spherical shell with \(\sigma(\theta)=k\cos\theta\), take the limit \(R\to 0\) and show that the result reproduces the delta-function term in the exact dipole field.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.50
problems.statement: (a) Prove Green's reciprocity theorem: if rho _1 produces V_1 and rho _2 produces V_2 , then int rho _1 V_2\,d tau = int rho _2 V_1\,d tau . Use two evaluations of int E _1 cdot E _2\,d tau and integration by parts. (b) Apply reciprocity to two separated conductors to show that if charge Q on conductor a produces potential V_ ab on conductor b , then the same charge on b produces the same potential V_ ba =V_ ab on a .
problems.raw_statement: (a) Prove Green's reciprocity theorem: if \(\rho_1\) produces \(V_1\) and \(\rho_2\) produces \(V_2\), then \(\int \rho_1 V_2\,d\tau = \int \rho_2 V_1\,d\tau\). Use two evaluations of \(\int \mathbf{E}_1\cdot\mathbf{E}_2\,d\tau\) and integration by parts. (b) Apply reciprocity to two separated conductors to show that if charge \(Q\) on conductor \(a\) produces potential \(V_{ab}\) on conductor \(b\), then the same charge on \(b\) produces the same potential \(V_{ba}=V_{ab}\) on \(a\).

problems.title: Griffiths 3.51
problems.statement: Use Green's reciprocity theorem to solve two induced-charge problems. (a) A point charge q lies between the grounded plates of a parallel-plate capacitor; find the total induced charge on each plate in terms of the plate spacing d and the charge position x . (b) A point charge q lies between two grounded concentric conducting spheres of radii a and b ; find the total induced charge on each sphere.
problems.raw_statement: Use Green's reciprocity theorem to solve two induced-charge problems. (a) A point charge \(q\) lies between the grounded plates of a parallel-plate capacitor; find the total induced charge on each plate in terms of the plate spacing \(d\) and the charge position \(x\). (b) A point charge \(q\) lies between two grounded concentric conducting spheres of radii \(a\) and \(b\); find the total induced charge on each sphere.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.52
problems.statement: (a) Rewrite the quadrupole contribution to the multipole expansion in tensor form and define the quadrupole moment tensor Q_ ij . (b) For the square of side a in the xy -plane centered at the origin, with corner charges -q,+q,+q,-q arranged as in Fig. 3.30, compute all nine components of Q_ ij . (c) Show that the quadrupole moment is independent of the choice of origin when both the monopole and dipole moments vanish. (d) Define the octopole moment and write the corresponding octopole term in the multipole expansion.
problems.raw_statement: (a) Rewrite the quadrupole contribution to the multipole expansion in tensor form and define the quadrupole moment tensor \(Q_{ij}\). (b) For the square of side \(a\) in the \(xy\)-plane centered at the origin, with corner charges \(-q,+q,+q,-q\) arranged as in Fig.~3.30, compute all nine components of \(Q_{ij}\). (c) Show that the quadrupole moment is independent of the choice of origin when both the monopole and dipole moments vanish. (d) Define the octopole moment and write the corresponding octopole term in the multipole expansion.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.53
problems.statement: Re-solve the problem of a conducting sphere in a uniform external electric field by the method of images, and verify that the result agrees with the solution previously obtained by separation of variables.
problems.raw_statement: Re-solve the problem of a conducting sphere in a uniform external electric field by the method of images, and verify that the result agrees with the solution previously obtained by separation of variables.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.54
problems.statement: In the infinite rectangular pipe, let the bottom y=0 and the side walls x= pm b be grounded, while the top y=a is held at constant potential V_0 . Find the potential inside. Set up the separation in the style of Example 3.4, include the k=0 mode, and work out the full solution.
problems.raw_statement: In the infinite rectangular pipe, let the bottom \(y=0\) and the side walls \(x=\pm b\) be grounded, while the top \(y=a\) is held at constant potential \(V_0\). Find the potential inside. Set up the separation in the style of Example 3.4, include the \(k=0\) mode, and work out the full solution.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.55
problems.statement: (a) A long square metal pipe of side a is grounded on three sides, while the fourth side is insulated and kept at constant potential V_0 . Find the net charge per unit length induced on the opposite side. (b) Repeat the question for a circular pipe of radius R split into four equal longitudinal conducting sections, three grounded and one held at V_0 ; find the charge per unit length on the opposite quarter.
problems.raw_statement: (a) A long square metal pipe of side \(a\) is grounded on three sides, while the fourth side is insulated and kept at constant potential \(V_0\). Find the net charge per unit length induced on the opposite side. (b) Repeat the question for a circular pipe of radius \(R\) split into four equal longitudinal conducting sections, three grounded and one held at \(V_0\); find the charge per unit length on the opposite quarter.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.56
problems.statement: An ideal electric dipole is fixed at the origin, pointing along the z -axis. A point charge is released from rest somewhere in the xy -plane. Show that its subsequent trajectory is a semicircle, like the motion of a pendulum whose support point is at the origin.
problems.raw_statement: An ideal electric dipole is fixed at the origin, pointing along the \(z\)-axis. A point charge is released from rest somewhere in the \(xy\)-plane. Show that its subsequent trajectory is a semicircle, like the motion of a pendulum whose support point is at the origin.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.57
problems.statement: A positive point charge q of mass m moves in a circular orbit of radius s in the field of a fixed dipole p =p hat z at the origin. Determine the orientation of the orbital plane and compute the speed, angular momentum, and total energy of the orbiting charge.
problems.raw_statement: A positive point charge \(q\) of mass \(m\) moves in a circular orbit of radius \(s\) in the field of a fixed dipole \(\mathbf{p}=p\hat{\mathbf{z}}\) at the origin. Determine the orientation of the orbital plane and compute the speed, angular momentum, and total energy of the orbiting charge.

problems.title: Griffiths 3.58
problems.statement: Find the surface charge density sigma ( theta ) on a sphere of radius R that reproduces, for all exterior points, the same field as a point charge q located on the z -axis at position a<R .
problems.raw_statement: Find the surface charge density \(\sigma(\theta)\) on a sphere of radius \(R\) that reproduces, for all exterior points, the same field as a point charge \(q\) located on the \(z\)-axis at position \(a<R\).

problems.title: Griffiths 4.1
problems.statement: A hydrogen atom, with Bohr radius about 0.5 AA , sits between two parallel metal plates separated by 1\, mm and connected to a 500\, V source. Estimate what fraction of the atomic radius the induced displacement d represents, and then estimate the voltage required to ionize the atom with that same apparatus. Use the polarizability listed in Table 4.1.
problems.raw_statement: A hydrogen atom, with Bohr radius about 0.5~\AA, sits between two parallel metal plates separated by \(1\,\mathrm{mm}\) and connected to a \(500\,\mathrm{V}\) source. Estimate what fraction of the atomic radius the induced displacement \(d\) represents, and then estimate the voltage required to ionize the atom with that same apparatus. Use the polarizability listed in Table 4.1.
problems.locator: Chapter 4: Electric Fields in Matter > Problem 4.1

problems.title: Griffiths 4.2
problems.statement: For a hydrogen atom in its ground state, the electron cloud has charge density rho (r)=q( pi a^3)^ -1 e^ -2r/a , where q is the electron charge and a is the Bohr radius. Compute the atomic polarizability. A suggested route is to first determine the field of the electron cloud and then expand for r ll a .
problems.raw_statement: For a hydrogen atom in its ground state, the electron cloud has charge density \(\rho(r)=q(\pi a^3)^{-1}e^{-2r/a}\), where \(q\) is the electron charge and \(a\) is the Bohr radius. Compute the atomic polarizability. A suggested route is to first determine the field of the electron cloud and then expand for \(r\ll a\).
problems.locator: Chapter 4: Electric Fields in Matter > Problem 4.2

problems.title: Griffiths 4.3
problems.statement: Equation 4.1 says that an induced dipole moment is proportional to the applied field. Test that rule for a model atom whose electron-cloud density grows in proportion to distance from the center up to radius R . Determine the power of E that appears in p(E) , and find the condition on rho (r) that guarantees a linear weak-field response.
problems.raw_statement: Equation 4.1 says that an induced dipole moment is proportional to the applied field. Test that rule for a model atom whose electron-cloud density grows in proportion to distance from the center up to radius \(R\). Determine the power of \(E\) that appears in \(p(E)\), and find the condition on \(\rho(r)\) that guarantees a linear weak-field response.
problems.locator: Chapter 4: Electric Fields in Matter > Problem 4.3

problems.title: Griffiths 4.4
problems.statement: A point charge q is placed far from a neutral atom with polarizability alpha . Find the attractive force between the charge and the atom.
problems.raw_statement: A point charge \(q\) is placed far from a neutral atom with polarizability \(\alpha\). Find the attractive force between the charge and the atom.
problems.locator: Chapter 4: Electric Fields in Matter > Problem 4.4

problems.title: Griffiths 4.5
problems.statement: Two perfect dipoles, mathbf p_1 and mathbf p_2 , are separated by distance r in the geometry shown in Fig. 4.6. Find the torque on mathbf p_1 due to mathbf p_2 , and the torque on mathbf p_2 due to mathbf p_1 , each taken about that dipole's own center.
problems.raw_statement: Two perfect dipoles, \(\mathbf p_1\) and \(\mathbf p_2\), are separated by distance \(r\) in the geometry shown in Fig.~4.6. problems.locator: Chapter 4: Electric Fields in Matter > Problem 4.5

problems.title: Griffiths 4.6
problems.statement: A perfect dipole mathbf p is located a height z above an infinite grounded conducting plane. The dipole makes angle theta with the normal to the plane. Find the torque on the dipole and determine the orientation of stable equilibrium if it is free to rotate.
problems.raw_statement: A perfect dipole \(\mathbf p\) is located a height \(z\) above an infinite grounded conducting plane. The dipole makes angle \(\theta\) with the normal to the plane. Find the torque on the dipole and determine the orientation of stable equilibrium if it is free to rotate.
problems.locator: Chapter 4: Electric Fields in Matter > Problem 4.6

problems.locator: Chapter 4: Electric Fields in Matter > Problem 4.7

problems.raw_statement: Derive the interaction energy of two dipoles separated by displacement \(\mathbf r\), expressing the answer in terms of \(\mathbf p_1\), \(\mathbf p_2\), and \(\hat{\mathbf r}\). Use the dipole-energy formula together with the electric field of a dipole.

problems.raw_statement: A dipole \(\mathbf p\) is located a distance \(r\) from a point charge \(q\), and \(\mathbf p\) makes angle \(\theta\) with the vector from \(q\) to the dipole. Find (a) the force on the dipole and (b) the force on the point charge.

problems.raw_statement: A short cylinder of radius \(a\) and length \(L\) carries a permanent uniform polarization parallel to its axis. Determine the bound charge distribution and sketch the field lines for the limiting cases \(L\gg a\), \(L\ll a\), and \(L\sim a\).

problems.raw_statement: A dielectric spherical shell with inner radius \(a\) and outer radius \(b\) has frozen-in polarization \(\mathbf P(\mathbf r)=(k/r)\hat{\mathbf r}\). There is no free charge. problems.raw_statement: Inside a large dielectric, the field is \(\mathbf E_0\) and the displacement is \(\mathbf D_0=\epsilon_0\mathbf E_0+\mathbf P\). problems.title: Griffiths 4.43
problems.statement: Derive the Langevin theory for a polar dielectric. problems.statement: Toy ring magnets can float on a vertical rod. Treat the magnets as dipoles of mass m_d and moment m . problems.title: Griffiths 6.24
problems.statement: Two charged magnetic dipoles, each with electric charge q and magnetic dipole moment m , are constrained to move on the z -axis. Electrical forces repel but magnetic dipole forces attract. Find the equilibrium separation, evaluate it for two electrons, and decide whether this implies a stable two-electron bound state.

problems.title: Griffiths 6.25
problems.statement: Use the dictionary mathbf D to mathbf B , mathbf E to mathbf H , mathbf P to mu _0 mathbf M , and epsilon _0 to mu _0 to translate known electrostatic results into magnetostatics. Apply it to rederive (a) the field inside a uniformly magnetized sphere, (b) the field inside a sphere of linear magnetic material placed in a uniform field, and (c) the average magnetic field over a sphere due to steady internal currents.

Apply it to rederive (a) the field inside a uniformly magnetized sphere, (b) the field inside a sphere of linear magnetic material placed in a uniform field, and (c) the average magnetic field over a sphere due to steady internal currents.

problems.title: Griffiths 6.26
problems.statement: Notice that uniformly charged, uniformly polarized, and uniformly magnetized bodies involve the same basic geometric integral. Use this observation to derive the scalar potential of a uniformly polarized sphere and the vector potential of a uniformly magnetized sphere from the corresponding uniformly charged-sphere result.

problems.title: Griffiths 6.28
problems.statement: A magnetic dipole mathbf m is embedded at the center of a sphere of radius R and permeability mu . Show that the magnetic field inside the sphere has the form stated in the text, including the added uniform term, and then determine the field outside the sphere.

problems.title: Griffiths 6.29
problems.statement: You are asked to referee a proposal aimed at deciding whether the magnetization of iron is better modeled by Ampere dipoles or Gilbert dipoles. The plan is to probe the interior field of a uniformly magnetized iron cylinder by carving a small cavity and placing a tiny compass needle inside. Decide whether such an experiment should be funded, and if so what cavity shape would be appropriate; if not, explain the flaw in the proposal.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.1
problems.statement: Two concentric conducting spherical shells of radii a and b are separated by weakly conducting material of conductivity sigma . Find the current for an applied potential difference V , determine the resistance between the shells, and explain why the answer becomes independent of b when b gg a . Then use that observation to find the current between two widely separated metal spheres of radius a immersed in seawater and held at potential difference V .
problems.raw_statement: Two concentric conducting spherical shells of radii \(a\) and \(b\) are separated by weakly conducting material of conductivity \(\sigma\). Find the current for an applied potential difference \(V\), determine the resistance between the shells, and explain why the answer becomes independent of \(b\) when \(b\gg a\). Then use that observation to find the current between two widely separated metal spheres of radius \(a\) immersed in seawater and held at potential difference \(V\).

problems.title: Griffiths 7.2
problems.statement: A capacitor C , initially charged to voltage V_0 , is connected at t=0 to a resistor R . Find Q(t) and I(t) , and verify that the heat dissipated in the resistor equals the loss of electrostatic energy. Then analyze the reverse process in which the capacitor and resistor are connected to a battery of emf V_0 : determine Q(t) , I(t) , the total battery work, the heat produced, the final stored energy, and the fraction of the battery's work that remains in the capacitor.
problems.raw_statement: A capacitor \(C\), initially charged to voltage \(V_0\), is connected at \(t=0\) to a resistor \(R\). Find \(Q(t)\) and \(I(t)\), and verify that the heat dissipated in the resistor equals the loss of electrostatic energy. Then analyze the reverse process in which the capacitor and resistor are connected to a battery of emf \(V_0\): determine \(Q(t)\), \(I(t)\), the total battery work, the heat produced, the final stored energy, and the fraction of the battery's work that remains in the capacitor.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.3
problems.statement: Two conductors are embedded in a weakly conducting medium of conductivity sigma . Show that the resistance R between them and the capacitance C of the same geometry satisfy R= epsilon _0/( sigma C) . Then suppose the conductors are first charged to a voltage V_0 and disconnected from the battery; show that the voltage decays exponentially, and find the time constant in terms of epsilon _0 and sigma .
problems.raw_statement: Two conductors are embedded in a weakly conducting medium of conductivity \(\sigma\). Show that the resistance \(R\) between them and the capacitance \(C\) of the same geometry satisfy \(R=\epsilon_0/(\sigma C)\). Then suppose the conductors are first charged to a voltage \(V_0\) and disconnected from the battery; show that the voltage decays exponentially, and find the time constant in terms of \(\epsilon_0\) and \(\sigma\).

problems.title: Griffiths 7.4
problems.statement: In the cylindrical resistor problem of Example 7.2, replace the constant conductivity by sigma (s)=k/s . Find the resistance between the cylinders, taking proper account of the fact that the electric field is no longer proportional to 1/s .
problems.raw_statement: In the cylindrical resistor problem of Example 7.2, replace the constant conductivity by \(\sigma(s)=k/s\). Find the resistance between the cylinders, taking proper account of the fact that the electric field is no longer proportional to \(1/s\).

problems.title: Griffiths 7.5
problems.statement: A battery of emf E and internal resistance r is connected to a variable load resistance R . Determine which choice of R delivers the greatest possible power to the load.
problems.raw_statement: A battery of emf \(\mathcal{E}\) and internal resistance \(r\) is connected to a variable load resistance \(R\). Determine which choice of \(R\) delivers the greatest possible power to the load.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.6
problems.statement: A rectangular wire loop is arranged so that one vertical side of height h lies between the plates of a parallel-plate capacitor, parallel to the electric field, while the rest of the loop lies in a region where the field is negligible. Find the emf around the loop and, if its total resistance is R , the current. Explain why the result does not lead to any perpetual-motion device.
problems.raw_statement: A rectangular wire loop is arranged so that one vertical side of height \(h\) lies between the plates of a parallel-plate capacitor, parallel to the electric field, while the rest of the loop lies in a region where the field is negligible. Find the emf around the loop and, if its total resistance is \(R\), the current. Explain why the result does not lead to any perpetual-motion device.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.7
problems.statement: A metal bar of mass m slides without friction on parallel conducting rails separated by distance l , with a resistor R closing the circuit and a uniform magnetic field B directed into the page. For a bar moving rightward at speed v , find the induced current and its direction, the magnetic force on the bar, the subsequent speed v(t) if released from speed v_0 at t=0 , and verify that the initial kinetic energy is entirely dissipated in the resistor.
problems.raw_statement: A metal bar of mass \(m\) slides without friction on parallel conducting rails separated by distance \(l\), with a resistor \(R\) closing the circuit and a uniform magnetic field \(B\) directed into the page. For a bar moving rightward at speed \(v\), find the induced current and its direction, the magnetic force on the bar, the subsequent speed \(v(t)\) if released from speed \(v_0\) at \(t=0\), and verify that the initial kinetic energy is entirely dissipated in the resistor.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.8
problems.statement: A square loop of side a lies on a table a distance s from an infinitely long straight wire carrying current I . Find the magnetic flux through the loop. Then determine the induced emf and current direction if the loop is pulled directly away from the wire at speed v , and repeat for the case in which the loop is translated parallel to the wire.
problems.raw_statement: A square loop of side \(a\) lies on a table a distance \(s\) from an infinitely long straight wire carrying current \(I\). Find the magnetic flux through the loop. Then determine the induced emf and current direction if the loop is pulled directly away from the wire at speed \(v\), and repeat for the case in which the loop is translated parallel to the wire.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.9
problems.statement: Explain why the magnetic flux through a wire loop is well defined even though infinitely many different surfaces can share the same boundary curve.
problems.raw_statement: Explain why the magnetic flux through a wire loop is well defined even though infinitely many different surfaces can share the same boundary curve.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.10
problems.statement: A square loop of side a rotates with angular speed omega about a vertical shaft in a uniform magnetic field B . Find the time-dependent emf E (t) produced by this alternating-current generator.
problems.raw_statement: A square loop of side \(a\) rotates with angular speed \(\omega\) about a vertical shaft in a uniform magnetic field \(B\). Find the time-dependent emf \(\mathcal{E}(t)\) produced by this alternating-current generator.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.11
problems.statement: A square loop cut from a thick aluminum sheet is placed so that its top section lies in a uniform magnetic field B directed into the page, and the loop is allowed to fall under gravity. Determine the terminal speed, the full velocity v(t) , and the time needed to reach 90\% of terminal speed. Then explain what changes if a narrow slit is cut in the loop so the circuit is broken.
problems.raw_statement: A square loop cut from a thick aluminum sheet is placed so that its top section lies in a uniform magnetic field \(B\) directed into the page, and the loop is allowed to fall under gravity. Then explain what changes if a narrow slit is cut in the loop so the circuit is broken.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.12
problems.statement: A long solenoid of radius a produces a sinusoidal interior field B(t)=B_0 cos ( omega t)\, mathbf z . A coaxial circular wire loop of radius a/2 and resistance R is placed inside the solenoid. Find the induced current as a function of time.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.13
problems.statement: A square loop of side a lies in the first quadrant of the xy -plane with one corner at the origin. In that region the magnetic field is mathbf B(y,t)=ky^3 t^2\, mathbf z . Compute the induced emf in the loop.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.14

problems.title: Griffiths 7.15

problems.statement: An alternating current I(t)=I_0 cos ( omega t) flows down a long straight wire and returns along a coaxial conducting tube of radius a . Determine the direction of the induced electric field and, assuming it vanishes as s to infty , find mathbf E(s,t) .

problems.title: Griffiths 7.17
problems.statement: A long solenoid of radius a and turn density n passes through a wire loop of resistance R . problems.raw_statement: A long solenoid of radius \(a\) and turn density \(n\) passes through a wire loop of resistance \(R\).
