problems.title: Griffiths 7.41
problems.statement: Two long straight copper pipes, each of radius a , are held a distance 2d apart in a weakly conducting medium of conductivity sigma . If one pipe is maintained at +V_0 and the other at -V_0 , find the current per unit length flowing from one to the other.
problems.raw_statement: Two long straight copper pipes, each of radius \(a\), are held a distance \(2d\) apart in a weakly conducting medium of conductivity \(\sigma\). If one pipe is maintained at \(+V_0\) and the other at \(-V_0\), find the current per unit length flowing from one to the other.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.42
problems.statement: An infinitely long cylindrical sheet of radius a has uniform surface resistivity and a slot acting as a battery so that V(a, phi )=V_0 phi /(2 pi ) for - pi < phi < pi . Use separation of variables in cylindrical coordinates to find the potential inside and outside the cylinder, and then determine the surface charge density on the sheet.
problems.raw_statement: An infinitely long cylindrical sheet of radius \(a\) has uniform surface resistivity and a slot acting as a battery so that \(V(a,\phi)=V_0\phi/(2\pi)\) for \(-\pi<\phi<\pi\). Use separation of variables in cylindrical coordinates to find the potential inside and outside the cylinder, and then determine the surface charge density on the sheet.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.43
problems.statement: A long straight wire of radius a carries steady current I , while the return path is a grounded perfectly conducting coaxial cylinder of radius b . Assume the outside potential is of the form V(s,z)=zf(s) . Solve for f(s) , determine the electric field in the region a<s<b , and calculate the surface charge density on the inner wire.
problems.raw_statement: A long straight wire of radius \(a\) carries steady current \(I\), while the return path is a grounded perfectly conducting coaxial cylinder of radius \(b\). Assume the outside potential is of the form \(V(s,z)=zf(s)\). Solve for \(f(s)\), determine the electric field in the region \(a<s<b\), and calculate the surface charge density on the inner wire.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.44
problems.statement: For a perfect conductor, show that the magnetic field inside is time independent and that the magnetic flux through any perfectly conducting loop is constant. For a superconductor, show that current is confined to the surface. Then consider a sphere of radius a cooled below its critical temperature while held in a uniform external field B_0 mathbf z ; find the induced surface current density as a function of polar angle.
problems.raw_statement: For a perfect conductor, show that the magnetic field inside is time independent and that the magnetic flux through any perfectly conducting loop is constant. For a superconductor, show that current is confined to the surface. Then consider a sphere of radius \(a\) cooled below its critical temperature while held in a uniform external field \(B_0\hat{\mathbf z}\); find the induced surface current density as a function of polar angle.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.45
problems.statement: Model a magnet levitating over an infinite superconducting half-space by a point magnetic dipole mathbf m fixed above the plane and constrained to point along mathbf z . Use the method of images to determine the image dipole orientation, the magnetic force on the real dipole, the floating height when that force balances gravity, and the induced surface current density on the superconductor.
problems.raw_statement: Model a magnet levitating over an infinite superconducting half-space by a point magnetic dipole \( \mathbf m\) fixed above the plane and constrained to point along \( \hat{\mathbf z}\). Use the method of images to determine the image dipole orientation, the magnetic force on the real dipole, the floating height when that force balances gravity, and the induced surface current density on the superconductor.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.46
problems.statement: If the levitating magnetic dipole of Problem 7.45 is free to rotate, determine the orientation it adopts and the height at which it floats above the superconducting plane.
problems.raw_statement: If the levitating magnetic dipole of Problem 7.45 is free to rotate, determine the orientation it adopts and the height at which it floats above the superconducting plane.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.47
problems.statement: A perfectly conducting spherical shell of radius a rotates with angular speed omega about the z -axis while immersed in a uniform magnetic field mathbf B=B_0 mathbf z . Find the emf between the north pole and the equator.
problems.raw_statement: A perfectly conducting spherical shell of radius \(a\) rotates with angular speed \(\omega\) about the \(z\)-axis while immersed in a uniform magnetic field \( \mathbf B=B_0\hat{\mathbf z}\). Find the emf between the north pole and the equator.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.48
problems.statement: Using the result of Problem 7.11 together with Problem 5.42, determine how long a falling circular ring of radius a , mass m , and resistance R takes to cross the lower edge of a magnetic-field region while moving at its changing terminal speed.
problems.raw_statement: Using the result of Problem 7.11 together with Problem 5.42, determine how long a falling circular ring of radius \(a\), mass \(m\), and resistance \(R\) takes to cross the lower edge of a magnetic-field region while moving at its changing terminal speed.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.49
problems.statement: Show that a Faraday-induced electric field can be written as mathbf E=- partial mathbf A/ partial t , and verify this by checking both the divergence and the curl. Then consider a uniformly charged spherical shell of radius R spinning about a fixed axis with slowly varying angular velocity omega (t) ; find the electric field inside and outside the shell, including both the Coulomb and induction contributions.
problems.raw_statement: Show that a Faraday-induced electric field can be written as \( \mathbf E=-\partial \mathbf A/\partial t\), and verify this by checking both the divergence and the curl. Then consider a uniformly charged spherical shell of radius \(R\) spinning about a fixed axis with slowly varying angular velocity \(\omega(t)\); find the electric field inside and outside the shell, including both the Coulomb and induction contributions.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.50
problems.statement: Electrons in a betatron gain energy from the induced electric field associated with a changing magnetic field. Show that the orbit radius can remain constant only if the average magnetic field over the orbit area is twice the magnetic field at the orbit itself.
problems.raw_statement: Electrons in a betatron gain energy from the induced electric field associated with a changing magnetic field. Show that the orbit radius can remain constant only if the average magnetic field over the orbit area is twice the magnetic field at the orbit itself.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.51
problems.statement: An infinite straight wire carrying steady current I along mathbf z moves in the y -direction with speed v . In the quasistatic approximation, find the electric field at the instant the wire coincides with the z -axis.
problems.raw_statement: An infinite straight wire carrying steady current \(I\) along \( \hat{\mathbf z}\) moves in the \(y\)-direction with speed \(v\). In the quasistatic approximation, find the electric field at the instant the wire coincides with the \(z\)-axis.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.52
problems.statement: An electron of charge q circles a nucleus of charge Q in a circular orbit of fixed radius r . A small magnetic field d mathbf B , perpendicular to the orbital plane, is turned on slowly. Show that the work done by the induced electric field changes the kinetic energy by exactly the amount required for the orbit to remain circular with the same radius.
problems.raw_statement: An electron of charge \(q\) circles a nucleus of charge \(Q\) in a circular orbit of fixed radius \(r\). A small magnetic field \(d\mathbf B\), perpendicular to the orbital plane, is turned on slowly. Show that the work done by the induced electric field changes the kinetic energy by exactly the amount required for the orbit to remain circular with the same radius.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.53
problems.statement: The magnetic flux through a long solenoid grows linearly with time, Phi = alpha t . Two ideal voltmeters, together with resistors R_1 and R_2 , are connected between diametrically opposite points A and B around the solenoid. Find the reading of each voltmeter.
problems.raw_statement: The magnetic flux through a long solenoid grows linearly with time, \( \Phi=\alpha t\). Two ideal voltmeters, together with resistors \(R_1\) and \(R_2\), are connected between diametrically opposite points \(A\) and \(B\) around the solenoid. Find the reading of each voltmeter.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.54
problems.statement: A circular wire loop of radius r and resistance R surrounds a region of uniform magnetic field that increases linearly with time. An ideal voltmeter is connected between two points P and Q on the loop. Find the loop current and the voltmeter reading.
problems.raw_statement: A circular wire loop of radius \(r\) and resistance \(R\) surrounds a region of uniform magnetic field that increases linearly with time. An ideal voltmeter is connected between two points \(P\) and \(Q\) on the loop. Find the loop current and the voltmeter reading.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.55
problems.statement: Revisit the motional-emf loop of Section 7.1.3, but now assume the wire is perfectly conducting, so R=0 , while the loop has finite self-inductance L . Show that the loop executes simple harmonic motion and determine its oscillation frequency.
problems.raw_statement: Revisit the motional-emf loop of Section 7.1.3, but now assume the wire is perfectly conducting, so \(R=0\), while the loop has finite self-inductance \(L\). Show that the loop executes simple harmonic motion and determine its oscillation frequency.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.56
problems.statement: Use the Neumann formula to compute the mutual inductance of the two-loop geometry of Fig. 7.37 in the limit a ll b and a ll z , and compare with the approximate result of Problem 7.22. Then, without assuming a is small, derive the expansion of the mutual inductance in powers of the dimensionless parameter beta =ab/(z^2+a^2+b^2) .
problems.raw_statement: Use the Neumann formula to compute the mutual inductance of the two-loop geometry of Fig.~7.37 in the limit \(a\ll b\) and \(a\ll z\), and compare with the approximate result of Problem 7.22. Then, without assuming \(a\) is small, derive the expansion of the mutual inductance in powers of the dimensionless parameter \(\beta=ab/(z^2+a^2+b^2)\).

problems.title: Griffiths 7.57
problems.statement: Two coils are wound on the same cylindrical form so that the same magnetic flux links every turn of both coils. If the primary has N_1 turns and the secondary has N_2 , show that the ratio of induced emfs satisfies mathcal E_2/ mathcal E_1=N_2/N_1 .
problems.raw_statement: Two coils are wound on the same cylindrical form so that the same magnetic flux links every turn of both coils. If the primary has \(N_1\) turns and the secondary has \(N_2\), show that the ratio of induced emfs satisfies \( \mathcal E_2/\mathcal E_1=N_2/N_1\).

problems.title: Griffiths 7.58
problems.statement: Analyze an ideal transformer. First show that M^2=L_1L_2 . Then let the primary be driven by V_ in =V_1 cos ( omega t) while the secondary is connected to a resistor R . Derive the coupled current equations, solve for I_1(t) and I_2(t) , show that the voltage ratio equals the turns ratio, and verify that the average input and output powers over a full cycle are equal.
problems.raw_statement: Analyze an ideal transformer. First show that \(M^2=L_1L_2\). Then let the primary be driven by \(V_{\mathrm{in}}=V_1\cos(\omega t)\) while the secondary is connected to a resistor \(R\). Derive the coupled current equations, solve for \(I_1(t)\) and \(I_2(t)\), show that the voltage ratio equals the turns ratio, and verify that the average input and output powers over a full cycle are equal.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.59
problems.statement: An infinite wire on the z -axis carries current I(z) , independent of time, and charge density lambda (t) , independent of z . Use charge conservation to show that lambda (t)=kt and I(z)=-kz , given lambda (0)=0 and I(0)=0 . Then show that the Coulomb and Biot-Savart expressions for the fields are in fact exact by checking Maxwell's equations.
problems.raw_statement: An infinite wire on the \(z\)-axis carries current \(I(z)\), independent of time, and charge density \(\lambda(t)\), independent of \(z\). Use charge conservation to show that \(\lambda(t)=kt\) and \(I(z)=-kz\), given \(\lambda(0)=0\) and \(I(0)=0\). Then show that the Coulomb and Biot-Savart expressions for the fields are in fact exact by checking Maxwell's equations.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.60
problems.statement: Suppose mathbf J( mathbf r) is time independent but rho ( mathbf r,t) is not. Show from the continuity equation that the charge density must depend linearly on time at each point. Then verify that, in this special case, the Biot-Savart expression for mathbf B satisfies the full Ampere-Maxwell equation.
problems.raw_statement: Suppose \(\mathbf J(\mathbf r)\) is time independent but \(\rho(\mathbf r,t)\) is not. Show from the continuity equation that the charge density must depend linearly on time at each point. Then verify that, in this special case, the Biot-Savart expression for \(\mathbf B\) satisfies the full Ampere-Maxwell equation.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.61
problems.statement: Recover the magnetic field of an infinite straight wire from Maxwell's displacement-current term alone by modeling the wire as moving line charge with a tiny gap. Compute the z -component of the electric field in the plane of a circular Amperian loop, determine its flux through the loop, and show that the resulting displacement current approaches the true current as the gap width goes to zero.
problems.raw_statement: Recover the magnetic field of an infinite straight wire from Maxwell's displacement-current term alone by modeling the wire as moving line charge with a tiny gap. Compute the \(z\)-component of the electric field in the plane of a circular Amperian loop, determine its flux through the loop, and show that the resulting displacement current approaches the true current as the gap width goes to zero.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.62
problems.statement: A transmission line consists of two thin metal ribbons of width w , separated by a small distance h ll w . The current travels down one ribbon and returns along the other. Find the capacitance per unit length and the inductance per unit length, determine the product LC in vacuum, and then repeat for the case in which the space between the ribbons is filled with material of permittivity epsilon and permeability mu . Interpret the resulting propagation speed.
problems.raw_statement: A transmission line consists of two thin metal ribbons of width \(w\), separated by a small distance \(h\ll w\). The current travels down one ribbon and returns along the other. Find the capacitance per unit length and the inductance per unit length, determine the product \(LC\) in vacuum, and then repeat for the case in which the space between the ribbons is filled with material of permittivity \(\epsilon\) and permeability \(\mu\). Interpret the resulting propagation speed.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.63
problems.statement: Prove Alfven's theorem for a perfectly conducting fluid: the magnetic flux through any closed loop moving with the fluid remains constant. First show that partial mathbf B/ partial t= nabla times ( mathbf v times mathbf B) when sigma to infty and mathbf J stays finite. Then analyze the changing flux through a moving loop, including the ribbon swept out in time dt , and use Stokes' theorem to complete the proof.
problems.raw_statement: Prove Alfven's theorem for a perfectly conducting fluid: the magnetic flux through any closed loop moving with the fluid remains constant. First show that \(\partial \mathbf B/\partial t=\nabla\times(\mathbf v\times \mathbf B)\) when \(\sigma\to\infty\) and \(\mathbf J\) stays finite. Then analyze the changing flux through a moving loop, including the ribbon swept out in time \(dt\), and use Stokes' theorem to complete the proof.

problems.title: Griffiths 7.64
problems.statement: For Maxwell's equations generalized to include magnetic charge, show that the equations are invariant under the duality rotation that mixes mathbf E with c mathbf B and electric charge with magnetic charge. Then show that the corresponding generalized Lorentz force law is invariant under the same transformation.
problems.raw_statement: For Maxwell's equations generalized to include magnetic charge, show that the equations are invariant under the duality rotation that mixes \(\mathbf E\) with \(c\mathbf B\) and electric charge with magnetic charge. Then show that the corresponding generalized Lorentz force law is invariant under the same transformation.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.1
problems.statement: Compute the power carried down the transmission lines of Example 7.13 and Problem 7.62, assuming the conductors are held at potential difference V and carry current I down one conductor and back along the other.
problems.raw_statement: Compute the power carried down the transmission lines of Example 7.13 and Problem 7.62, assuming the conductors are held at potential difference \(V\) and carry current \(I\) down one conductor and back along the other.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.2
problems.statement: Return to the charging capacitor model of Problem 7.34. Find the electric and magnetic fields in the gap as functions of radius s and time t . Then compute the electromagnetic energy density and Poynting vector, paying attention to the direction of energy flow, and verify the local conservation law. Finally, determine the total energy stored in the gap and show that the net Poynting flux into the gap equals the rate at which that energy increases.
problems.raw_statement: Return to the charging capacitor model of Problem 7.34. Find the electric and magnetic fields in the gap as functions of radius \(s\) and time \(t\). Then compute the electromagnetic energy density and Poynting vector, paying attention to the direction of energy flow, and verify the local conservation law. Finally, determine the total energy stored in the gap and show that the net Poynting flux into the gap equals the rate at which that energy increases.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.3
problems.statement: Use Maxwell's stress tensor to calculate the magnetic force of attraction between the northern and southern hemispheres of a uniformly charged spherical shell that spins with angular velocity omega .
problems.raw_statement: Use Maxwell's stress tensor to calculate the magnetic force of attraction between the northern and southern hemispheres of a uniformly charged spherical shell that spins with angular velocity \(\omega\).

problems.title: Griffiths 8.4
problems.statement: Consider two point charges separated by distance 2a . Integrate Maxwell's stress tensor over the plane midway between them to find the force of one charge on the other when the charges are equal, and repeat when the charges have opposite signs.
problems.raw_statement: Consider two point charges separated by distance \(2a\). Integrate Maxwell's stress tensor over the plane midway between them to find the force of one charge on the other when the charges are equal, and repeat when the charges have opposite signs.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.5
problems.statement: Two infinite parallel sheets carry surface charges + sigma and - sigma and move side by side with speed v . Find the electromagnetic momentum in a region of area A . Then let the upper sheet move slowly downward until it reaches the lower one, and show that the impulse delivered during the collapse equals the momentum originally stored in the fields.
problems.raw_statement: Two infinite parallel sheets carry surface charges \(+\sigma\) and \(-\sigma\) and move side by side with speed \(v\). Find the electromagnetic momentum in a region of area \(A\). Then let the upper sheet move slowly downward until it reaches the lower one, and show that the impulse delivered during the collapse equals the momentum originally stored in the fields.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.6
problems.statement: A charged parallel-plate capacitor with uniform field mathbf E=E mathbf z is placed in a uniform magnetic field mathbf B=B mathbf x . Find the electromagnetic momentum between the plates. Then connect the plates with a resistive wire along the z -axis so the capacitor discharges slowly, and determine the total impulse delivered to the system.
problems.raw_statement: A charged parallel-plate capacitor with uniform field \( \mathbf E=E\hat{\mathbf z}\) is placed in a uniform magnetic field \( \mathbf B=B\hat{\mathbf x}\). Find the electromagnetic momentum between the plates. Then connect the plates with a resistive wire along the \(z\)-axis so the capacitor discharges slowly, and determine the total impulse delivered to the system.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.7
problems.statement: For an infinite parallel-plate capacitor with plate surface charges pm sigma , compute all nine components of the Maxwell stress tensor in the region between the plates. Use the result to find the force per unit area on the upper plate and the momentum flux through a plane between the plates. Then imagine the supporting insulator is removed so this momentum flux is absorbed by the plates, and compare the resulting force with the earlier answer.
problems.raw_statement: For an infinite parallel-plate capacitor with plate surface charges \(\pm\sigma\), compute all nine components of the Maxwell stress tensor in the region between the plates. Use the result to find the force per unit area on the upper plate and the momentum flux through a plane between the plates. Then imagine the supporting insulator is removed so this momentum flux is absorbed by the plates, and compare the resulting force with the earlier answer.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.8
problems.statement: In Example 8.4, instead of reducing the solenoid current to turn off the magnetic field, connect a weakly conducting radial spoke between the cylinders so the electric field is discharged away. Determine the total angular momentum transferred to the cylinders from the magnetic force on the spoke current, and compare it with the angular momentum originally stored in the fields.
problems.raw_statement: In Example 8.4, instead of reducing the solenoid current to turn off the magnetic field, connect a weakly conducting radial spoke between the cylinders so the electric field is discharged away. Determine the total angular momentum transferred to the cylinders from the magnetic force on the spoke current, and compare it with the angular momentum originally stored in the fields.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.9
problems.statement: Two concentric spherical shells of radii a and b>a carry charges +Q and -Q , respectively, and are immersed in a uniform magnetic field mathbf B=B_0 mathbf z . Find the angular momentum stored in the fields. Then gradually switch off the magnetic field and compute the torque on each shell and the resulting angular momentum of the matter.
problems.raw_statement: Two concentric spherical shells of radii \(a\) and \(b>a\) carry charges \(+Q\) and \(-Q\), respectively, and are immersed in a uniform magnetic field \( \mathbf B=B_0\hat{\mathbf z}\). Find the angular momentum stored in the fields. Then gradually switch off the magnetic field and compute the torque on each shell and the resulting angular momentum of the matter.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.10
problems.statement: An iron sphere of radius R carries total charge Q and a uniform magnetization mathbf M=M mathbf z . First compute the angular momentum stored in the electromagnetic fields. Then study two different ways of releasing that angular momentum: gradual demagnetization of the sphere, and slow discharge through a grounding wire attached at the north pole. In each case, find the torque and the total angular momentum imparted to the sphere.
problems.raw_statement: An iron sphere of radius \(R\) carries total charge \(Q\) and a uniform magnetization \( \mathbf M=M\hat{\mathbf z}\). First compute the angular momentum stored in the electromagnetic fields. Then study two different ways of releasing that angular momentum: gradual demagnetization of the sphere, and slow discharge through a grounding wire attached at the north pole. In each case, find the torque and the total angular momentum imparted to the sphere.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.11
problems.statement: Derive Eq. 8.39 for the upward magnetic force in the two-loop lifting model by treating the lower loop as a magnetic dipole.
problems.raw_statement: Derive Eq.~8.39 for the upward magnetic force in the two-loop lifting model by treating the lower loop as a magnetic dipole.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.12
problems.statement: Derive Eq. 8.43 for the magnetic energy of two coupled current loops by building both currents up gradually from zero to their final values.
problems.raw_statement: Derive Eq.~8.43 for the magnetic energy of two coupled current loops by building both currents up gradually from zero to their final values.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.13
problems.statement: A very long solenoid of radius a and turn density n carries current I_s . Coaxial with it, at radius b gg a , is a circular conducting ring of resistance R . When the solenoid current is slowly reduced, find the induced current in the ring. Then evaluate the Poynting vector just outside the solenoid and integrate it over the solenoid surface to confirm that it accounts for the power dissipated in the ring.
problems.raw_statement: A very long solenoid of radius \(a\) and turn density \(n\) carries current \(I_s\). Coaxial with it, at radius \(b\gg a\), is a circular conducting ring of resistance \(R\). When the solenoid current is slowly reduced, find the induced current in the ring. Then evaluate the Poynting vector just outside the solenoid and integrate it over the solenoid surface to confirm that it accounts for the power dissipated in the ring.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.14
problems.statement: An infinitely long cylindrical tube of radius a moves at constant speed v along its axis and carries uniform line charge density lambda on its surface. A coaxial outer cylinder at radius b moves with the same speed and carries charge density - lambda . Find the field energy per unit length, the field momentum per unit length, and the rate at which the fields transport energy across a plane perpendicular to the axis.
problems.raw_statement: An infinitely long cylindrical tube of radius \(a\) moves at constant speed \(v\) along its axis and carries uniform line charge density \(\lambda\) on its surface. A coaxial outer cylinder at radius \(b\) moves with the same speed and carries charge density \(-\lambda\). Find the field energy per unit length, the field momentum per unit length, and the rate at which the fields transport energy across a plane perpendicular to the axis.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.15
problems.statement: A point charge q sits at the center of a tightly wound toroidal coil of rectangular cross section, inner radius a , outer radius a+w , height h , and total current I . Assuming w,h ll a , find the electromagnetic momentum of the configuration. Then suppose the current is turned off quickly enough that the charge hardly moves, and show that the impulse delivered to the charge equals the initial field momentum.
problems.raw_statement: A point charge \(q\) sits at the center of a tightly wound toroidal coil of rectangular cross section, inner radius \(a\), outer radius \(a+w\), height \(h\), and total current \(I\). Assuming \(w,h\ll a\), find the electromagnetic momentum of the configuration. Then suppose the current is turned off quickly enough that the charge hardly moves, and show that the impulse delivered to the charge equals the initial field momentum.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.16
problems.statement: A sphere of radius R carries a uniform polarization mathbf P and a uniform magnetization mathbf M , not necessarily parallel. Find the total electromagnetic momentum stored in the fields.
problems.raw_statement: A sphere of radius \(R\) carries a uniform polarization \( \mathbf P\) and a uniform magnetization \( \mathbf M\), not necessarily parallel. Find the total electromagnetic momentum stored in the fields.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.17
problems.statement: Model the electron as a uniformly charged spherical shell of radius R rotating with angular velocity omega . Compute the total electromagnetic energy and total electromagnetic angular momentum. Then impose the assumptions U_ em =m_ec^2 and L_ em = hbar /2 to infer R , omega , and the product omega R , and assess whether the classical picture is self-consistent.
problems.raw_statement: Model the electron as a uniformly charged spherical shell of radius \(R\) rotating with angular velocity \(\omega\). Compute the total electromagnetic energy and total electromagnetic angular momentum. Then impose the assumptions \(U_{\mathrm{em}}=m_ec^2\) and \(L_{\mathrm{em}}=\hbar/2\) to infer \(R\), \(\omega\), and the product \(\omega R\), and assess whether the classical picture is self-consistent.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.18
problems.statement: Generalize the formulas for electromagnetic energy density, Poynting vector, momentum density, and Maxwell stress tensor to the case where magnetic charge and magnetic current are allowed.
problems.raw_statement: Generalize the formulas for electromagnetic energy density, Poynting vector, momentum density, and Maxwell stress tensor to the case where magnetic charge and magnetic current are allowed.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.19
problems.statement: Consider an electric charge q_e and a magnetic monopole q_m separated by a distance d . Compute the total angular momentum stored in the electromagnetic fields.
problems.raw_statement: Consider an electric charge \(q_e\) and a magnetic monopole \(q_m\) separated by a distance \(d\). Compute the total angular momentum stored in the electromagnetic fields.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.20
problems.statement: Show that an ideal stationary magnetic dipole mathbf m placed in a static electric field mathbf E carries electromagnetic field momentum mathbf p=- epsilon _0 mu _0( mathbf m times mathbf E) .
problems.raw_statement: Show that an ideal stationary magnetic dipole \(\mathbf m\) placed in a static electric field \(\mathbf E\) carries electromagnetic field momentum \( \mathbf p=-\epsilon_0\mu_0(\mathbf m\times \mathbf E)\).

problems.title: Griffiths 8.21
problems.statement: In Example 8.4 the cylinders retain angular velocity after the solenoid current has been extinguished, so a residual magnetic field and residual field angular momentum remain. Compute that final field angular momentum in terms of the cylinder angular velocities, and determine the additional angular momentum transferred through the extra azimuthal electric field induced by the changing magnetic field of the rotating cylinders.
problems.raw_statement: In Example 8.4 the cylinders retain angular velocity after the solenoid current has been extinguished, so a residual magnetic field and residual field angular momentum remain. Compute that final field angular momentum in terms of the cylinder angular velocities, and determine the additional angular momentum transferred through the extra azimuthal electric field induced by the changing magnetic field of the rotating cylinders.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.22
problems.statement: A point charge q lies a distance a>R from the axis of an infinite solenoid of radius R , turn density n , and current I . Find the linear momentum and the angular momentum, about the origin, stored in the electromagnetic fields.
problems.raw_statement: A point charge \(q\) lies a distance \(a>R\) from the axis of an infinite solenoid of radius \(R\), turn density \(n\), and current \(I\). Find the linear momentum and the angular momentum, about the origin, stored in the electromagnetic fields.

problems.title: Griffiths 8.23
problems.statement: Rework the energy and momentum conservation arguments in matter using only the free charge and free current densities. Show that the Poynting vector becomes mathbf S= mathbf E times mathbf H , derive the corresponding field-energy density, and then show that the electromagnetic momentum density takes the form mathbf g= mathbf D times mathbf B .
problems.raw_statement: Rework the energy and momentum conservation arguments in matter using only the free charge and free current densities. Show that the Poynting vector becomes \( \mathbf S=\mathbf E\times \mathbf H\), derive the corresponding field-energy density, and then show that the electromagnetic momentum density takes the form \( \mathbf g=\mathbf D\times \mathbf B\).

problems.title: Griffiths 8.24
problems.statement: A circular disk of radius R and mass M carries n identical point charges q equally spaced around its rim. At t=0 the disk lies in the xy -plane, spins with angular velocity omega _0 , and is then released in the external magnetic field mathbf B(s,z)=k(-s\, mathbf s +2z\, mathbf z ) . Find the vertical motion z(t) of the center and the angular velocity omega (t) , describe the motion qualitatively, and confirm that the total kinetic energy remains constant.
problems.raw_statement: A circular disk of radius \(R\) and mass \(M\) carries \(n\) identical point charges \(q\) equally spaced around its rim. At \(t=0\) the disk lies in the \(xy\)-plane, spins with angular velocity \(\omega_0\), and is then released in the external magnetic field \( \mathbf B(s,z)=k(-s\,\hat{\mathbf s}+2z\,\hat{\mathbf z})\). Find the vertical motion \(z(t)\) of the center and the angular velocity \(\omega(t)\), describe the motion qualitatively, and confirm that the total kinetic energy remains constant.
